content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To  : File                                    Date  : July 21, 1980


                                              A-80-07-076 •

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dwight Dickerson

Subject: Letter from Jim Richman dated March 5, 1980


I responded by telephone on July 16, 1980, to Jim Richman's
request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act. Mr. Richman asked if it is a con-
flict of interest for a councilman to vote for a non-binding
resolution that puts the city council on record in opposition to
rent control.

I referred Mr. Richman to the Ferraro Opinion, 4 FPPC Ops.
62 (No. 78-009, Nov. 7, 1978), which provides that if an official
owns three or fewer rental units he or she need not be disqualified
from a rent control matter because of his or her interest in
rental property. The Commission's conclusion in Ferraro rests
on the belief that an official with three or fewer units will
not be affected by a rent control matter in a manner that is
distinguishable from the public generally.

In those instances where an official owns more than three
units, an analysis must be made as to whether the decision will
have a foreseeable material financial effect on the official's
interests. I indicated to Mr. Richman that such a determination
cannot be made in a vacuum. Such determinations can only be
made when there is a sufficiently concrete proposal of rent
control that can be identified with the resolution pending before
the council. The adoption of a resolution expressing the official's
general views regarding rent control is not enough standing
alone to justify a conclusion that the Act's Conflict of Interest
provisions have been violated. This is particularly true when
it is considered that the council is not bound by the resolution.

DED:nrw


                                              87703(b)

sections.question: Is it a conflict of interest for a councilman to vote for a non-binding resolution that puts the city council on record in opposition to rent control?
sections.conclusion: The adoption of a resolution expressing the official's general views regarding rent control is not enough standing alone to justify a conclusion that the Act's Conflict of Interest provisions have been violated. This is particularly true when it is considered that the council is not bound by the resolution. An official with three or fewer rental units need not be disqualified from a rent control matter. Where an official owns more than three units, a concrete proposal of rent control must be identified with the resolution pending before the council to determine whether there is a foreseeable material financial effect.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilman has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when voting on a non-binding resolution opposing rent control.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A non-binding resolution expressing general opposition to rent control does not violate conflict of interest provisions. Officials with three or fewer rental units are not disqualified from rent control matters, and those with more units require analysis of whether a concrete rent control proposal would have a foreseeable material financial effect.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is telephone advice memorandum dated July 21, 1980, responding to March 5, 1980 inquiry. No formal Q/A sections present; question and conclusion extracted from narrative text. References Ferraro Opinion (4 FPPC Ops. 62, No. 78-009). Document appears to be internal FPPC memo rather than formal advice letter format.

QUESTION: Is it a conflict of interest for a councilman to vote for a non-binding resolution that puts the city council on record in opposition to rent control?

CONCLUSION: The adoption of a resolution expressing the official's general views regarding rent control is not enough standing alone to justify a conclusion that the Act's Conflict of Interest provisions have been violated. This is particularly true when it is considered that the council is not bound by the resolution. An official with three or fewer rental units need not be disqualified from a rent control matter. Where an official owns more than three units, a concrete proposal of rent control must be identified with the resolution pending before the council to determine whether there is a foreseeable material financial effect.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a city councilman's vote on a non-binding resolution opposing rent control does not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, citing the Ferraro Opinion and the requirement for a sufficiently concrete proposal to trigger conflict analysis.
